Hi Brian. Using that code you pasted, I saved it as 'issue16.py' and then ran it as:

```
./issue16.py irc.quakenet.org jaraco
```

When I did, it appeared to connect and proceed as expected. Because it doesn't output any content, I used Wireshark to [trace the connection](http://paste.jaraco.com/file/txFsU), and I could see the client connect, set a nick, join the channel, and respond to PINGs with PONGs (every 180 sec). I let it run for more than 10 minutes without a problem (although the trace only shows the first 6.5 min).

This leads me to believe you may have an issue in your network environment that's causing your TCP connections to drop. I've witnessed for example ComCast dropping idle SSH connections after as little as 30 seconds.

If it is as I expect something in your network environment, you may need to add a PeriodicCommand to send a keepalive message of some kind. I'm not familiar enough with the protocol, but I'm guessing there's a client PING command or equivalent that could be configured as a PeriodicCommand.

When you run that script, what behavior do you get that's undesirable? Can you paste the offending output? Also, if possible, could you use tcpdump or WireShark to capture the packets when it fails, which might also shed some light on the situation?

If we can confirm it's an idle timeout issue, then we can probably put together a client-side ping for keepalive purposes. I remember another request for this feature in the past, so it's probably worth implementing in general.

Partially.  After reviewing the discussion, I realize that my comment is a bit off topic.  I ran into a similar issue trying to debug a script that uses relative imports.  According to [PEP 366](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0366/), the loader is supposed to set the `__package__` variable to `None` when a script is directly executed.  Thus, when run as `python script.py`, `__package__` is supposed to be `None`; whereas, it is properly set when run as `python -m script`.  The Python 3 version of `pdb` does not do this.  Rather, it sets `__package__` to the empty string.  I haven't fully reviewed your source, but I suspect this is affecting the relative import in your case too.  The minimum working example I attached to the linked issue above shows this behavior in both `pdb` and `ipdb`.

I like the idea of supporting the -m flag, but if the erroneous loader behavior is present in Python, you would have to manually work around it. I opened the issue on the Python project because it seems to me it should be handled at the underlying source.

If you want to catch the `ZeroDivisionError` you should pass `reraise=True` to `@retry()`.

Otherwise you should catch `tenacity.RetryError`.

Note that using reraise in your case will work as expected because the only exception is `ZeroDivisionError`. In the case where different exceptions types could cause a retry, this is more risky.

What about predefined models like what exists in Matlab : https://mathworks.com/help/curvefit/list-of-library-models-for-curve-and-surface-fitting.html

For example :
```python
from symfit import parameters, Parameter, variables, Variable, Fit, Model, exp

def GaussianModel(n=1):
    x = Variable(name='x')
    model = 0
    var = ('a', 'b', 'c')
    for i in range(n):
        a, b, c = parameters(','.join(['{}{}'.format(var[j], i+1) for j in range(len(var))]))
        model += a*exp(-((x-b)/c)**2)
    
    return model

def PolynomialModel(order1, order2=None):
    if order2 == None:
        x = Variable(name='x')
        model = 0
        
        for i in range(order1+1):
            p = Parameter(name='p{}'.format(i+1))
            model += p * x**(order1-i)
                           
        return model
    else:
        x, y, z = variables('x, y, z')
        max_order = max(order1, order2)
        model = 0

        for i in range(order1+1):
            for j in range(order2+1):
                if i+j <= max_order:
                    p = Parameter(name='p{}{}'.format(i,j))
                    model += p * x**i * y**j
                           
        return Model({z: model})
```

Same outcome also running `pip3 show pyowm` with `sudo` ?

Here my best guess is that your OSX actually has **two** Python3.6 interpreters along with their respective library folders, and modules get installed in different locations depending on the one you use. In your case, when you originally installed PyOWM with `pip3`, that was using an interpreter which was different than the one you ran when you after tried to import PyOWM.

This could be explained if you installed a new Python setup using Homebrew or similar package managers on OSX

Please run:

```
$ for b in `compgen -A command python3.6`; do     echo "Command: $b - $(which $b)"; done
```
and post back here, we should see the 2 binaries and their location.

Also these pages might be helpful to give you an idea of the issue (the 1st one also proposing a solution):
  - https://stackoverflow.com/questions/9532703/mismatch-in-modules-installed-with-python
  - https://stackoverflow.com/questions/42477834/python-3-6-path-confusion

Sure thing. I didn't want to pollute this space too much at the start. I don't think there's any need to bring over the test directory. I simply used it a sandbox to investigate things.

This directory serves to illustrate the problem and fix for building shared
libraries with cuppa. Two files, f1.cpp and f2.cpp, are compiled and linked
into a shared library, libfoo.so, which is then used by the executable, bar,
created from bar.cpp.

First build using scons alone and verify the executable works:
$ scons -f SConstruct_scons
$ LD_LIBRARY_PATH='.' ./bar

Notice the following:
- All generated files are dumped in this directory.
- The "-fPIC" option is passed to the compiler for f1.cpp and f2.cpp.
- The object file suffix is ".os" for f1.os and f2.os.
- The build works.
- The executable works.

Now clean up:
$ scons -f SConstruct_scons -c
$ rm .sconsign.dblite

Now try to build using cuppa:
$ scons

Notice the following:
- All the genereated files are under _build. (Nice!)
- The "-fPIC" options is NOT passed to the compiler for f1.cpp and f2.cpp.
- The file suffix is ".o" for f1.o and f2.o.
- The link of the shared library fails with a scons error message about
  static object files not being compatible with shared target:

    """
    scons: *** [_build/gcc54/dbg/x86_64/c++1z/final/libfoo.so] Source file:
     _build/gcc54/dbg/x86_64/c++1z/working/_build/gcc54/dbg/x86_64/c++1z/working/f1.o
     is static and is not compatible with shared target:
     _build/gcc54/dbg/x86_64/c++1z/final/libfoo.so
    scons: building terminated because of errors.
    """

This error comes from /usr/lib/scons/SCons/Defaults.py:120 in the function
SharedFlagChecker. I found StackOverflow suggestions to define
STATIC_AND_SHARED_OBJECTS_ARE_THE_SAME in the build environment, but that
really has a bad code smell. It is defined for the 'aix' platform, for
example, in /usr/lib/scons/SCons/Tool/g++.py:61 but that whole thing
looks like a kludge.

You can see that the SharedFlagChecker passes if the target has an attribute
'shared' which should be set by the SharedObjectEmitter defined at
/usr/lib/scons/SCons/Default.py:106 and added to the shared object builder at
/usr/lib/scons/SCons/Tool/c++.py:71. Yes, this file appears to be just for the
IBM VisualAge C++ compiler according to its comments, but it gets brought into
/usr/lib/scons/SCons/Tool/g++.py at line 45 and used at line 56.

The shared object builder is originally created in the function
createObjBuilders from /usr/lib/scons/SCons/Tool/__init__.py:720. This gets
called at /usr/lib/scons/SCons/Tool/g++.py:51 and again, unnecessarily, at
/usr/lib/scons/SCons/Tool/c++.py:65 via the call at g++.py:56.

So...

Clean up again:
$ rm .sconsign.dblite config.log
$ rm -rf _build .sconf_temp

and apply the fix to the following files:
- cuppa/methods/build_library.py
- cuppa/methods/compile.py
where ever your cuppa is installed.

Now build using cuppa:
$ scons
$ ./_build/gcc54/dbg/x86_64/c++1z/final/bar

Your path may differ depending on your toolset. (... as if you didn't know!)

Notice the following:

I think this is a better fix than defining STATIC_AND_SHARED_OBJECTS_ARE_THE_SAME.
That prevents an important test from happening. This fix to cuppa passes the
corrent intention from cuppa to SCons; cuppa's BuildSharedLib method uses Scons'
SharedObject builder. It also means that if a user builds individual components
of a shared library manually using SCons' SharedObject builder that cuppa will
recognize them in the source list passed to BuildSharedLib. Without the fix,
the CompileMethod callable would not recognize such objects as SCons would give
them the SHOBJSUFFIX and cuppa would be testing against the OBJSUFFIX.

It is an entirely different discussion whether SCons should rely on the
SharedObjectEmitter and object attributes to determine if an object file is
relocatable or not. It could instead, and perhaps more reliably, use something
like the 'file' command on *nix operating systems, but then I suppose they
would have to track down the correct thing to do for all operating systems.

Original comment by: Tarek Ziadé

In #106, @dhellmann suggests (and sends a [corresponding pull request](/kang/python-keyring-lib/pull-request/34)) that the requirement simply be removed.

However, it's not so simple to simply remove the requirement because it's inconvenient. The purpose of the requirement, as described above, is to enable a person or system to be able to invoke the tests without having to have anything special in his environment (except Python and setuptools).

The setup_requires declaration takes advantage of the setuptools machinery to ensure that packages necessary to invoke the full operation of the setup script are available. It does this for any environment and without any prerequisite steps. It's these advantages that are particularly valuable because it factors out those prerequisite steps into a single, declarative indicator. If this declaration were removed, it would require (a) the documentation to be updated to indicate how to set up an environment to test, (b) the travis CI tests (and any other automated test scripts) to be updated to fulfill this need, and (c) testers would be responsible for creating a suitable environment in which to meet these prerequisites, running the tests, and then resetting the environment to the state prior to the tests. The whole testing process becomes a lot more complicated without support for downloading the requirements in advance.

This need would be obviated if there were a way to invoke py.test from setuptools without the 'setup_requires' declaration (like there is for nose and unittest), but I don't believe there is.

We can work around the issue by suppressing the presence of 'pytest-runner' in the setup_requires unless 'ptr' is in the command-line. It's a bit of a hack, and inelegant, but far superior to simply removing it.

prometheanfire indicated that the issue 'is not problemmatic', so I considered the issue low priority. The other issue to consider here is I'd like to add another 'setup_requires' requirement to keyring to enable mercurial repository support and version tagging (via [hgtools](/jaraco/hgtools)). If the presence of a pytest-runner requirement is indicative of a general problem with setup_requires dependencies, then I'd like to explore solving that problem in general, rather than simply suppressing it for this particular case.

The callback system for pydash doesn't work the same way as callbacks passed to `map()`:

```python
>>> import pydash
>>> items = [1, 2, 3, 4]
>>> callback = lambda val: val * 2
>>> list(map(callback, items))
[2, 4, 6, 8]
>>> pydash.map_(items, callback)
[2, 4, 6, 8]
>>> import pprint
>>> callback = lambda item, key, obj: (item, key, obj)
>>> pprint.pprint(pydash.map_(items, callback))
[(1, 0, [1, 2, 3, 4]),
 (2, 1, [1, 2, 3, 4]),
 (3, 2, [1, 2, 3, 4]),
 (4, 3, [1, 2, 3, 4])]
>>> callback = lambda item, key, obj: (item, key)
>>> pydash.map_(items, callback)
[(1, 0), (2, 1), (3, 2), (4, 3)]
```

You can also do things like:

```python
items = [{'a': 1}, {'a': 2}, {'a': 3}, {'a': 4}]
>>> pydash.map_(items, 'a')
[1, 2, 3, 4]
>>> pydash.map_(items, {'a': 1})
[True, False, False, False]
>>> pydash.map_(items, {'a': 2})
[False, True, False, False]
>>> pydash.map_(items, {'a': 3})
[False, False, True, False]
>>> pydash.map_(items, {'a': 4})
[False, False, False, True]
```

Basically you can pass in a callbacks with differing number of args (and as objects or strings) and pydash will call them appropriately (this is done to simulate the JS-style function arguments and to make it easier to use builtins without having to define wrappers [e.g. ``pydash.map(items, lambda value, *args: str(value))`` vs ``pydash.map(items, str)``]. Originally pydash required all callbacks to have the correct number of args but I decided to relax that requirement to make the callbacks easier to use while sacrificing performance.

The slowness in pydash typically comes from the callback system. You can read some discussion on that in #58 along with some changes that were done at the time to address some of the performance issues. I'm open to other ideas around improving performance though.

Unfortunately, in my years of developing Cpppo, the documentation available for EtherNet/IP CIP itself, and for most devices has been essentially useless.

Most of the time, I resort to using Wireshark or Ethereal or 'tcpdump' to trace the raw communications between two working devices, or between some working software and the target device.  Then, I try to manually parse the communications (or follow the Ethereal or Wireshark parse output (which often only partially decodes the communications, if at all).  Usually, that gives me the information I need to implement some more of the protocol.

So; if you have any software that successfully talks to the SoftLogix using "Unconnected" communications (eg. RSLogix), you should sniff its communications while it accesses those tags, and see what it's doing...

Sorry I can't be more helpful!  I've been shaking my head in disbelief for, literally, *years* while developing Cpppo...

Just enough depth to keep the device saturated with requests.  Mostly, depth is required due to slow (high latency) communications channels.

The multiple flag allows Cpppo to aggregate multiple requests into a single transmitted CIP request.  If the target device can handle it, it may increase the total number of I/O operations that can be handled, per second, by reducing the over head of decoding multiple separate requests.

So, simply by experimenting you should be able to find the optimal settings for your device.

Finally -- Cpppo is 100% pure Python.  Even the low-level parser is Python.  So, I have seen some noticable speed-ups by using PyPy, especially if your program runs for long periods of time (ie. performs some requests, waits then performs the operation again (without shutting down and restarting))

Making any language work with an automated documentation system is a "meet in the middle" situation between the tool and the programmer. The tool will make good faith efforts to parse everything properly and produce reasonable results, but the programmer also has to not go out of his or her way to make things obtuse. My statements above were not really clear enough.  In particular, it wasn't clear that the problem applies to *arrays* of quaternions rather than individual quaternions.  So I'll just write a simple test that shows the problem

```python
import numpy as np
import quaternion

q = np.array([np.quaternion(1,2,3,4), np.quaternion(5,6,7,8)])
q_c = np.array([np.quaternion(1,-2,-3,-4), np.quaternion(5,-6,-7,-8)])

assert np.array_equal(q.conjugate(), q_c)
```

The same is true for `;` .

The regex's are looking for whitespace before the comment start characters `#` and `;`.  If there is no whitespace before it, it fails to parse the comment.  Fixing that is easy...

However, it then means that there is no way to use `#` or `;` in a values.

One backwards compatible way of handling this is to support a stream reader parameter `comment_characters = '#;'`, so clients can choose which comment characters they prefer to have active, and possible also a config variable `comment_characters = #;` which the stream reader will detect and then use until it appears again in the config file.

I had the same problem when using variable sized inputs. 
The problem comes from `vis.optimizers.Optimizer._get_seed_input(self, seed_input)`, in
```
if seed_input.shape != desired_shape:
    seed_input = np.moveaxis(seed_input, -1, 1)
```
The problem is that in Keras, variable shaped entries in the model are defined as `None`, which compared to an integer results in `False`.

For example the model input could have shape `(None, None, 224, 224, 3)` when using 224 x 224 RGB videos as input, while the input data has shape `(1, 10, 224, 224,3)` for a video of length 10. This will make `seed_input.shape != desired_shape` evaluate to `True`, which results in swapping the seed input channel axis with `np.moveaxis(seed_input, -1, 1)`

My quickfix was just to comment out the two lines in `vis.optimizer`
```
# if seed_input.shape != desired_shape:
    # seed_input = np.moveaxis(seed_input, -1, 1)
```

Here is a short sample code:
```
from pptx import Presentation
from lxml import etree
from pptx.opc.constants import RELATIONSHIP_TYPE as RT

prs = Presentation("original.pptx")
self.delete_slides(prs, enumerate(prs.slides), 0)  # This is a separate method that deletes all slides

view_props = prs.part.part_related_by(RT.VIEW_PROPS)
tree = etree.fromstring(view_props.blob)  # I get the blob XML string and parse it with etree
elements = tree.xpath('.//*[local-name()="sldLst"]')  # I get the conflicting elements

for element in elements:
    element.getparent().remove(element)  # I get rid of the conflicting elements

# Then I convert the modified XML back to string and assign it to the blob attrib 
view_props.blob = etree.tostring(tree, xml_declaration=True, encoding="UTF-8", standalone="yes")
prs.save("blank.pptx")
```

There's 2-main styles of async: futures (also referred to as promises) and callbacks. Python in general seems to prefer the futures route. In general it results in much easier to follow code and avoids "callback-hell", but there's a time and place for both.

The critical bit with future objects is that they're a handle to the result. You get them back immediately while the work happens in the background and can go about doing other things until you need the result. At that point you can get at it with future.resp() or yeild future.

You can take a look at the README for dirt simple examples and [a blog post I wrote around the same time](https://www.xormedia.com/high-performance-web-asynchronous-http/) for how this works specifically with requests futures. For a more general discussion of futures see https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Futures_and_promises.

output = [int(x.split()[2].strip())

Resolution:

In 0.11, when mxnet was built with CUDA enabled, OMP was not used for most operations in mxnet, even on CPU-only operations.  This changed in 0.12.

In 1.0, two additional things happened:
  1) @piiswrong refactored broadcast operators from using the deprecated mshadow framework to his Kernel/Launch framework
  2) Auto-tuning for selecting the most optimal OMP or non-OMP modes based upon thread count, number of data items and operator calculation "weight", thus using OMP for larger datasets and single-thread for smaller datasets (which is often faster).

Post 1.0, IntelOMP is built and used by default for cmake builds.

These things together make mxnet faster than numpy for most broadcast ops (so far as I have seen so far).  Times I get for the above script:

PASS 1...
(1000, 10000)
avg time over 100 trials: 0.0125194501877s
(1000, 10000)
avg time over 100 trials: 0.0115594983101s
PASS 2...
(1000, 10000)
avg time over 100 trials: 0.0119210195541s
(1000, 10000)
avg time over 100 trials: 0.0114824414253s

So, I think that the issue is resolved in current builds.  To get similar performance in your 0.11, you'd probably need to build without CUDA enabled.  

Please reopen the issue if necessary.

- Having a single graph plus conditionals
- Producing different graphs one after the other by building and closing sessions

Here's what I propose:

* Continue raising `WaiterError` to keep backwards compatibility
* Add a `last_response` attribute on a waiter error that contains the last response we received before raising a `WaiterError`

The rationale behind just adding a `last_response` instead of exposing some sort of `error_code` and `error_message` is that a `WaiterError` can also be thrown in non-error states (transitioning to a `failure` state, hitting `max-attempts`), and while we can always guarantee a last response, we can't necessarily guarantee that response is a "error" response.

w = ec2.get_waiter('instance_running')
try:
    w.wait(InstanceIds=['i-1234'])
except WaiterError as e:
    if 'Error' in e.last_response:
        print "Received an error:", e.last_response['Error']['Code']
```

The correct way to install from the lock file (without any input from Pipfile) is actually `pipenv sync`. The logic works like this:

* The user edits Pipfile to express what is wanted for the app
* `pipenv lock` takes that the user wants (from Pipfile), and resolve them into locked dependencies (Pipfile.lock)
* `pipenv sync` takes the locked dependencies (from Pipfile.lock), and install them into the environment.

`pipenv install` (without other arguments) is essentially `lock` + `sync`, so it is more like *installing from Pipfile) instead.

I would also like to mention that documentation contributions are very welcomed.

I ran into the same problem on Ubuntu with Pipenv 10.1.0.

I tracked the problem down to [the command](https://github.com/christoomey/vim-tmux-navigator/blob/d724094e7128acd7375cc758008f1e1688130877/vim-tmux-navigator.tmux#L3-L4) being run to determine if vim is open in the active tmux pane:

```
is_vim="ps -o state= -o comm= -t '#{pane_tty}' \
    | grep -iqE '^[^TXZ ]+ +(\\S+\\/)?g?(view|n?vim?x?)(diff)?$'
```

You can see the problem by runing the following from a tmux pane with vim open: 

```
:run-shell "ps -o state= -o comm= -t '#{pane_tty}'"
```

If pipenv shell is not activated, the output is:

```
S bash
S vim
```

if pipenv shell *is* activated, the output is:

```
S bash
S pipenv
```

So the grep that looks for `vim` is failing.

I've [worked around this problem](https://github.com/RobbieClarken/dotfiles/commit/c1a8e28e46e4b0d26f6778e11fec3cea16e526e1) by creating a script called `tmux-check-if-vim` with the following body:

```
#!/bin/bash
pane_tty=$1
pipenv_pid=$(pgrep -t ${pane_tty/\/dev\/} pipenv)
if [[ $? == 0 ]]; then
  ps -o state=,comm= -p $(pstree -p $pipenv_pid | grep -Po '\(\d+\)' | tr -d '()')
else
  ps -o state=,comm= -t $pane_tty
fi | grep -qP '^[^TXZ] (view|vim)$'
```

and replacing the `is_vim` variable in `~/.tmux.conf` with

```
is_vim="tmux-check-if-vim #{pane_tty}" 
```

The script first looks for a `pipenv` process running in the active pane. If it finds one, it looks for `vim` running as a descendant process of pipenv. Otherwise it looks for vim as a top-level process running in the pane as per normal.

Note the grep I do to look for vim-like programs is less comprehensive than the one suggested by vim-tmux-navigator. In particular, it doesn't try to catch nvim, gvim, vimx, etc. This shouldn't be difficult to add.

Also, I have only tested on Linux so it is quite likely it won't work out of the box on macOS if grep, ps, pgrep or pstree behave differently.

It works sort like how you check you downloaded the right file from, say, python.org. You download a file [listed in the table](https://www.python.org/downloads/release/python-365/), and after you’re done, check if the file’s actual hash matches the one listed in the table. `pipenv lock` saves all the hashes in the table locally ahead of time, so it doesn’t need to check online every time you create a new environment (and in some circumstances it can’t), but the idea is the same. It ensures this current download matches the information it got when you lock.

This is my two cents:
1. Making virtualenv movable make it easier in deployment from development, to test, and into production envrionment.  Cheap as it may be to create, you have to reinstall packages that take time.  If you have hundreds of server servers to install and maintain, that can take its toll.  Maintaining one virtualenv and distributing it to servers is a safer way.
2. We are doing all the time, creating virtualenv.  Then, modifying its scripts to be movable.  Then we have a single environment that we know is right, and we ship that environment to all server.
3. others are doing it too.  Just search the web for issue in relocatable virtualenv activate.bat or bash and you will find plenty that overcome this issue.  So solving it in the core can benefit everyone.

All right @scikit-image/core! This one needs some decidin'. It turns out that our existing `moments` code uses xy coordinates, where for nD we would really want to use rc coordinates. This would normally be a straightforward deprecation (see code), but it turns out that image moments are used in a *lot* of regionprops. It's a deprecation *nightmare* to keep them all using xy. So my vote is to break the API and use rc throughout — at which point the deprecation might itself be worth skipping.

One big, massive hair in the ointment is orientation. Using the straightforward definition of the moments and of the orientation, it is now tilted by 90º relative to the usual xy orientation. This is the cause of the last remaining failing test. A horizontal line, which normally would have had orientation of 0, now has orientation of 90º. This makes sense when you think that the first axis (r) should be considered to have an orientation of 0.

Now, I'm among the most gung-ho supporters of rc coordinates (see above), but setting a vertical orientation as 0 is a bit much even for me. Should we maintain horizontal (right-facing) as the 0º orientation?

Hi @lumpofcole!

It looks to me like there might be some issues with the way you installed Cython. Sometimes Python can't find locally installed libraries, and you have to manually set up various environment variables, such as PYTHONPATH, to point Python to them.

The first thing I would say is that you can install local files with pip, and probably should. Instead of `python setup.py build; python setup.py install --user`, do `pip install --user .`. You can even use this for binary wheels downloaded directly from PyPI.

Secondly, I recommend working with an environment manager, which should make things like dealing with Python paths less painful. See the Python Packaging user guide:
https://packaging.python.org/

I personally use [conda](http://conda.pydata.org/miniconda.html), but many use virtualenv or venv (these are, confusingly, two different tools) (see Python Packaging, above). The point of these is that they allow user-space install, while correctly setting up PYTHONPATH, LD_LIBRARY_PATH, and other environment variables for you.

I don't know exactly where your install is going wrong, but I hope the above tools and links will help. Please report back here if any of the above tools solved your problem! Or ask again if they didn't.

> Does it mean that for an Object-Object tree, the max amount of values to be set on the tree is 30*250? 

IIRC the node sizes given are the maximum before the node *splits* into another level, thus impacting the height of the tree. Nodes split automatically when needed and AFAIK there is no artificial maximum number of keys one tree can hold.

> possibly large sorted list of objects indexed by a datetime value

My app has a similar index. We turn the datetimes into their floating point timestamps so we can use an optimized tree (actually, we turn them into an integer representation of their floating point timestamp so we can normalize and use an even more optimized tree; this is a [common approach](https://github.com/zopefoundation/Products.ZCatalog/tree/master/src/Products/PluginIndexes/DateIndex), although it may not directly work for you).

Depth parameter is about how many levels deep you make the query (0 is only the brain of the given path, 1 is it and its children) : http://docs.plone.org/develop/plone/searching_and_indexing/query.html?highlight=querying#query-by-path
The level parameter is the level from which you search the level path. For example, `catalog(path=dict(query='some/path', level=2))` searches for all objects whose path contains some/path at level 2. This includes paths like /foo/bar/some/path and /spam/eggs/some/path, plus all children of those paths.

The plone documentation does not refer to the level parameter. You have to look at Products.ExtendedPathIndex readme to get information on it : https://github.com/plone/Products.ExtendedPathIndex/blob/master/README.txt

After looking into it and following the winding codebase, it seems like android-fastboot essentially does this:

* Call `getvar max-download-size` to get the target's max size.
* If the result (`max_size`) is greater than the filesize, then just flash it normally.
* Call `s = sparse_file_import_auto(fd, check_crc=false, verbose=true)` on the file descriptor
* Call `count = sparse_file_resparse(s, max_size, out_s=nullptr, count=0)` to get the size of `out_s`
* Allocate `out_s` as a list of `struct sparse_file` of size `count`
* Call `sparse_file_response(s, max_size, out_s, count)`
  * Now the file is in a list of `struct sparse_file`s called `out_s`. 
* Rip them into a list of (`sparse_file`, block size) pairs by calling `sparse_file_len(s, sparse=true, crc=false)`
* For each pair:
  * Immediately forget that we got the size and get the size again (ugh...) with `sparse_file_len(s, sparse=true, crc=false)`
  * Send a `download:%(block_size)s` with that block size
  * Call `sparse_file_callback(s, sparse=true, crc=false, write=callback, priv=transport)` which calls the callback as `callback(transport, data, len)`
  * In `callback`, concatenate the data and send it in chunks of 1024 bytes
  * Send the last chunk, if any, of bytes (< 1024 by definition)
  * Send a `flash:%(partition)s` for the current partition

So that's how it's done. It looks like `libsparse` is pulled out into a separate `deb` file in newer Debian and Ubuntu distributions, but it seems we'll need to ship a copy internally for now.

> If you look e.g. at pandas or seaborn, most of the user-facing functions are in the main name-space.

This is my least favorite thing about the pydata ecosystem.  It makes it a pain to find where things are actually defined, which often contributes to circular dependencies which get resolved with runtime imports rather than proper package structure (which in turn bites us on Quantopian because we run in a chroot for security reasons), and it means that performance issues in **any** of your dependencies leak into module in your code, which creates an unpleasant user experience.

I'm not arguing that there's no place for putting important functionality in a top-level namespace, but we should be careful about what we choose to export in this way, and we should be aware of the performance and maintenance costs that come from this.

I'd like to get a better understanding of your use case.  I've got a working branch on which retrieving the allowed values list on a custom attribute that is created as a "Drop Down List (Multi Value)" which in turn is identified from WSAPI as being of type "COLLECTION" is supported.  I've adjusted some of the internal mechanism to disqualify many standard attributes of the same type (COLLECTION) from chasing the collection url to get the "allowed values".  The rationale for the latter is that most of the standard attributes of COLLECTION type are not really workspace wide value collections, but rather dependent on the specific artifact, in which case it is less expensive to just get the specific attribute value off of the item as opposed to getting the schema item and running the machinery to get the allowed values for every field.

Alternate theory: this error occurs because you are finding a Google server which doesn't speak the right SSL protocol.

From an openssl [mailing list][1]:
> Versions in client/server SSL records do not agree.
> Probably your client sends SSL2 ... and server is configured only for SSL3/TLS1.
> In this situation server does not accept SSL2 ... is being manifested by "wrong version number" error.
> To resolve this error you may disable SSL2 on client or enable SSL2 handshake on server.
> tcpdump output from wrong session handshake may be helpful too.

> what's the use case? just incremental `binary` goal build perf for pants?

Yeah, I was looking into pex resolve issues for an internal pants-plugin I was playing with (which no longer exists) and got sidetracked and learned some more about how pants does python.

> ultimately, it's not clear to me how to best combine 1..N pex files into a net new pex without running another top level resolve against the new transitive closure of deps - which I thought was most of the runtime cost for the incremental build case.

I actually wasn't quite clear on the mechanism being described here -- I think I can see how just extending the PEX_PATH could make that resolution process much longer (especially if it's already most of the runtime cost of incremental build, that's a fun fact).

> it's worth noting that PEX_PATH remains a "best effort" thing given the proclivity for conflicts between various pex files 3rdparty requirements. IIRC, we effectively just bootstrap each pex in order and layer on the sys.path mutations. this seems fine for controlled runtime cases, but for sealed binary builds maybe less so?

Moving the native backend to contrib was based off a discussion with @stuhood that happened haphazardly in #engine and #native -- there are two concerns here. The first is that we're unconditionally pulling down gcc and clang right now for building any `python_dist()`, regardless of whether that `python_dist()` has native sources -- that is an artifact of a (hasty) implementation, and is fixed by #5815, so it's not a reason to move anything to contrib by itself, but once it was mentioned I realized that it would be very easy to move the entirety of the native backend into contrib, and it provided an excuse to test adding `@rule`s to plugin `register.py`s. I think it would make a lot of sense to move `python_dist()` into contrib as well, since the way it stitches things into the build graph means there's not any new product dependencies to declare. It also seems to make sense to me (and this is what @stuhood was saying) that because (at least right now) we *can* split this functionality off into contrib pretty cleanly, it would make sense to do so for the same reasons we have anything else in contrib. But I'm not familiar with the expectations/guidelines/etc about what should or shouldn't be in contrib so if that sounds bogus it doesn't need to happen!

Hi thanks for reaching out!

The reason the include transform works for `AWS::Serverless::Api` is because the yaml/JSON in the `DefinitionBody` parameter gets passed through to the [Body](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-resource-apigateway-restapi.html#cfn-apigateway-restapi-body) property of the `AWS::ApiGateway::RestApi` resource type, which supports a yaml or JSON object embedded in the template itself.

The [DefinitionString](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-resource-stepfunctions-statemachine.html#cfn-stepfunctions-statemachine-definitionstring) property of the `AWS::StepFunctions::StateMachine` resource type expects a string containing a JSON definition of the statemachine. However, the include transform attempts to embed the statemachine definition as an embedded yaml/JSON object rather than a string containing the JSON statemachine definition.

I don't know of a way to use the include transform to accomplish this. Here are some options I can think of:

1. Embed the statemachine as a string in the template.
1. Use a template parameter to hold the statemachine definition and pass the statemachine in as the parameter value when creating/updating the stack.
1. Use a templating system to construct the template at build-time so you can store/develop the statemachine in a separate file, but still embed it into the template before passing it to CloudFormation.

@HarshTrivedi, thanks for helping!  What you said is _almost_ right.  You conflated the field name and the field's namespace.  Instead of

```python
fields['ny_namespace'] = LabelField(label)
```

you want

```python
fields['my_field_name'] = LabelField(label, label_namespace='my_namespace')
```

Everything else you said looked right to me (oh, and default non-padded namespaces are things that end in "tags" or "labels", not "tag" or "label").

The field names that you use are arbitrary; you just have to make sure that wherever they are read in code that you're using matching field name names (and most often this is just in [the arguments to the `forward` method](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/77298a975a8edf8032723a140f4137a0fd326e61/allennlp/models/decomposable_attention.py#L97-L100)).

The _field name_ is just used for matching inputs in the dataset reader with inputs in your model code.  The _vocabulary namespace_ is used to create separate embedding matrices for separate _kinds_ of input.  You could have multiple fields that use the same vocabulary; e.g., if you have a `Field` for a question, and a `Field` for a paragraph, as in SQuAD, they should probably share the same vocabulary.  The _field names_ here would be `"question"` and `"paragraph"`, where the _vocabulary namespace_ for both would be something like `"tokens"`.

I think you're seeing and responding to the fact that the activation model we have is confusing. Which it is. I wouldn't try to pretend otherwise.

We know we need to completely redesign activation at some point in order to fix it or at least make it simpler, but it's a pretty difficult task. I don't know / can't tell when we'll be able to address it.

> three of the endpoints above were active but returned as activated False in the initial queries

This sounds like you're conflating endpoint activation -- the binding of a credential for a specific user to a specific endpoint -- with the existence/validity of an endpoint.

The Transfer service has no notion of an endpoint being valid or invalid, functional or broken.
Both the service used for activation and the GridFTP servers which handle data transfers and filesystem interactions are only known to work or fail at the time that a user tries to use them.

Knowing that an endpoint is correctly configured and operational is a more nuanced and treacherous road than it may at first appear. The API avoids presenting misleading or stale information by following an EAFP design.

---

If I were to recommend a way of presenting this information to users, it would be to follow the lead of the Globus web app. Our current view for an [endpoint search](https://www.globus.org/app/endpoints) has columns like so:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1300022/38620792-70afe65a-3d6d-11e8-8ad7-e82a8b9d92c0.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1300022/38620757-5d8a507e-3d6d-11e8-9fd4-725b2103f7b0.png)

These columns are primarily populated from the `activated` and `expires_in` fields of the endpoint documents.
"setup incomplete" is an interesting status produced from `is_globus_connect` (the endpoint is a Globus Connect Personal installation) and `globus_connect_setup_key` being non-null.


Though you may choose different phrasing, stating that an endpoint is "not activated" hasn't generated much user confusion for us, anecdotally.

To hypothesize a little bit: most users probably assume that everything they're being shown is valid/works. Pairing the activation status, "not active", with some kind of actionable response, the "activate" link, is probably worth imitating because it reinforces that thinking.

### Most user friendly way to do this.

You'll need the middleware from #649 .  With that you can load the private key for the account you're trying to sign into that middleware and it will intercept the `sendTransaction` call, sign it, and send it as a raw transaction.

### Less UX friendly way: option A

Use infura for *read* operations.  Build the transaction object using `contract.functions.theFunction(param, param2).buildTransaction({"from": ...})` to create an unsigned transaction dictionary.

Then using `w3.eth.account` API load your private key in and use the transaction signing API on the account object.  The signed transaction will have a `rawTransaction` attribute that you can then send over `w3.eth.sendRawTransaction`

### Less UX friendly option B

Use a local node with the appropriate private key unlocked, call code like *normal*.

You could also lean on this mixin that I made: https://bitbucket.org/ospreyinformatics/django-hstore-mixin/ .  It's not ideal but it works well enough (I use it on a few projects).  It's what led me to make that PR.  The goal is that, once that PR goes through, this mixin will be obsolete and all the data in the DB will be serialized, making it compatible with the new `SerializedDictionaryField`.

But this is something that confuses me: the `AngleLimitRegisterController` controller is a "get" controller - meaning it synchronises the values from the servo to the logical motor. But those values never change on their own, like say present position, voltage, temperature, etc. Instead these values have to be set explicitly by the program, much like return delay time, max torque and indeed the ID. 

So, is it not more natural to use the setter methods in the servos (DxlMotor, etc...) to call the io to set the limits? And that's it. The constructors already receive the limits and call the setters - so that will automatically take care of the initialisation. We can remove completely that obscure check_motor_eprom_configuration called in from_json (it took me 2 hours to figure out where the limits are set...). And also if you need during operation to change the limits: just call the setter for the motor. I find it more natural.

I think there is a significant difference between CADD and frequencies. 
A non existing frequency have a meaning, namely that the variant most probably have a very low frequency which most of the time means that it is very interesting to us.
A non existing CADD score most probably mean that the variant is a SV since it should be calculated for all possible SNV:s in the entire genome.
The problem with including NA CADD variants is that if you set the filter high the result will almost only include SV:s which will clog the result.
Is it not better to use another filter to see those?

I should make it more clear that `--quiet` isn't the same thing as no output.  The `--quiet` flag suppresses stdout output, but not log output.  If you do not specify a log file, then Curator defaults to use stdout as its logging target.  This is likely what you're experiencing: stdout log output.

If you want it to be completely quiet, you will need to use a `--logfile`.  Without it, you'll still be logging to stdout, hence output.

Why would I have both stdout _and_ logfile output?  So that users will get some messages at the command-line no matter what (except in the case of the new `--quiet` flag).

I don't think there's an official doc, but here's the description of the fields:

- `source`: the source in [sources](https://github.com/openaddresses/openaddresses/tree/master/sources)
- `cache`: the URL for the cached copy of the raw source data 
- `sample`: 5 sample records from the latest machine run
- `geometry type`: the geometry type of the source (usually one of `Point`, `Polygon`, or `Polygon`)
- `address count`: the number of addresses processed (and available in the output) from the latest machine run
- `version	fingerprint`: unique hash code for the source run
- `cache time`: the amount of time spent caching the source data
- `processed`: the URL for the processed results from the machine run
- `process time`: the amount of time spent processing the cached data
- `process hash`: hash of the contents of the processed data
- `output`: machine output for the source run
- `attribution required`: `true` or `false` denoting whether using the data requires attribution
- `attribution name`: text of the attribution to use when `attribution required` is `true`
- `share-alike`: `true`, `false`, or blank when source requires [share alike licensing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Share-alike)
- `code version`: the tagged machine version used to process the source

Ran into this same problem on OS X 10.11 and did a bit of digging. Here's my hypothesis about the root cause:

On OS X, the file `distutils.cfg` (located somewhere under `Python.framework`) defines the variable `include_dirs` for the `build_ext` command. When the `build_ext` command is initialized, its `include_dirs` attribute is set from this config, which later (in `build_ext.finalize_options()` causes the `include_dirs` attribute on the `Distribution` instance (you know, the one created by calling `setup(...)`) to be *completely ignored*.

On Windows, I have no `distutils.cfg`. I wonder what the situation is like on a Linux.

Are you running the notebook server on SSH and then disconnecting?

There are two things that can happen to cause this:

- The contents of `/run/user/1000` (aka XDG_RUNTIME_DIR) can be deleted after 6 hours if they don't have the 'sticky bit' set. We [try to set the sticky bit](https://github.com/jupyter/jupyter_client/blob/44980c13680f4e4226cf25f199ce4e4bb6e11296/jupyter_client/connect.py#L140-L164), but maybe that's going wrong somewhere.
- `/run/user/1000` itself has to be deleted when you log out. The definition of 'log out' is not really clear, but in at least some circumstances, starting jupyter in screen or nohup and then disconnecting seems to cause it to disappear.

I had this issue.  In my particular case, I was analyzing an APK that I had broken apart, made changes, and rebuilt.  I found out that in the session object's `add` method calls `androconf.is_android_raw()`.

`is_android_raw()` looks at the first few characters to determine the type of the file.  BUT, if it starts with `"PK"` (i.e. it's a zip), it also looks for the string `"META-INF/MANIFEST.MF"` in the data.  That file did not exist in the APK I was analyzing, I had to sign it with a private key to make the tools create that directory in the ZIP.  Then `AnalyzeAPK` worked fine.

Suggestion: 
1. Open the APK as a ZIP, 
1. look for the existence of 
   1. directory META-INF, and 
   1. the file MANIFEST.MF within it.
1. If they don't exist, sign the APK 

Hopefully, that helps a bit.

This compiler had Span support added later than CoreCLR. As a result the newer version is needed.

I put together [this](https://gist.github.com/svonohr/44aa4ccbdb13f875a40da0e3adb60796) proof of concept. Some details are still open for debate:
- Do we use AES-128, AES-256 or allow it to be user selectable? Note, that AES-256 isn't necessarily more secure, just because there is a bigger number. Even Bruce Schneier [recommends](https://www.schneier.com/blog/archives/2009/07/another_new_aes.html) using AES-128 for new applications (at the moment the code still uses AES-256).
- Number of PBKDF2HMAC iterations: I've set this to 500k iterations, which takes approximately half a second on my 5 year old laptop. Do we make this option user selectable? Running 500k iterations on low power hardware (Raspberry Pi, etc...) might take several seconds. Note that an iteration count of 1 is sufficient for already cryptographically secure inputs.
- I've implemented filename encryption using CBC mode with the IV determined by a HMAC of the filename. This might not be the most secure way, but should be good enough for encrypting filenames. Because of the required padding in CBC mode, the exact filename length cannot be deduced from the ciphertext. The encrypted filename can be stored in the metadata and the actual filename should be the hash of the encrypted filename. I'm still asking myself how to handle folders. Do we even expose the folder structure on B2? 
I think that's most of it for now. I already mentioned that I'm not that familiar with Python, so there might be simpler or more portable ways of doing things.

> But the principal idea of it is that Layers would compute only a subset of their normal output, so it seems vital to be able to communicate a corresponding mask to later Layers.

Ah yes, I see. In a sequence of dense layers with batchwise dropout, a layer would use the previous layer's output mask to select the correct rows from its weight matrix, create an output mask to select the correct columns from its weight matrix, and pass on the output mask to the next layer.
In this use case, it's vital that the next layer understands how to use the mask (it should fail if it doesn't), and the mask is not supposed to be passed on indefinitely.

Theano's lack of multi-GPU support (for now) is the main thing holding it back in this respect. Also, people who work on convnets for computer vision seem to prefer other libraries (e.g. Caffe). Theano seems to be more popular in different deep learning subfields.

As a result, there is indeed very little work in terms of training these large-scale image classification models in Theano directly, but I don't think it's impossible. The fact is that these models are a lot less demanding in terms of the types of neural network building blocks they consist of, and all the more demanding in terms of raw training performance, so Theano might not be the best fit in that case.

That said, it definitely isn't impossible, and nothing stops you from implementing custom optimized ops for certain operations yourself. I believe this is currently being done for batch normalization: https://github.com/Theano/Theano/pull/3410

This type of work is what many other libraries expect you to do all the time anyway. Theano's symbolic paradigm just makes rapid prototyping of new ideas a bit easier.

Besides GoogLeNet though, I don't really know of any 'complex' models that Theano is less suitable for. (OxfordNet shouldn't be a problem.) Do you have any more examples?

> Is there any reason not to require the hidden state to always be 2D, where the first dimension is either 1 (meaning "use the same initialization for all samples in the batch", which is the use-case the majority of the time), or `n_batch` (meaning "use a different initialization for each sample in the batch", arguably an edge-case)?

Yes, we cannot distinguish these two cases, unless we add an extra keyword to the constructor. All we can reliably check for a given `hid_init` is `ndim`. Maybe an extra constructor keyword would be better to distinguish the cases. If we add this, we could use it to exactly specify which axes the initial values are to be shared over -- e.g., for convolutional recurrent layers, one may want to have a single value per channel, not per spatial position. The solution entails hiding the TransposedConv3DLayer when it's unavailable, mocking it for Sphinx so it's still included in the documentation, and skipping the tests when it's unavailable: https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/pull/841/commits/d0a3916

You are looking at the solution as an integer while argv[1] it is actually a string. If you look at it as a string, and decode it, here is what you get:

```
>>> '2b30303030303133'.decode('hex')
'+0000013'
>>>
```

That said, I am not quite sure how state.se.min() behaves with strings exactly (I presume alphabetically?) and what you really want is probably to find the minimum value for the output of atoi(argv[1]) rather than argv[1] itself.

> which results in a size of 2. Due to distance being 3. Distance should be 4 according to IDA-Pro

This is indeed a bug, but it is because `CFGFast` does not take THUMB mode instructions into consideration when calculating the `distance`. So the correct fix would be detecting if the block is in THUMB mode, and if it is, decrement the `addr` by one before calculating the distance.

I guess the issue in this case is that it is a live stream and youtube doesn't stream separate audio versions of live video streams. If show_video is false we try to find audio streams if not fall back to video. This is why you're still seeing videos when you're trying to open this video.

Adding the --video=no flag would be a potential way around the issue. You would still have to spend the bandwidth to download the video, just not show it. Controlling this kind of behavior should be implemented in the GenericPlayer class and the player specific stuff would have to implemented in their respective player specific classes so we cover all the supported players.

The intended functionality would have to be something along the lines of that if `show_video` is false, look for audio stream or the best suited video stream.
If and only if it found a video and show_video is false, should it trigger the generic hide video field in the Player class that should append the correct video tag.

The synchronization between two streams from the same sensor like depth and IR should be perfectly synchronized by frame number.
Synchronization between two streams from separated sensors, like color and depth, is achieved by best match of the frames timestamp,
It’s expected that frames within a synchronized frameset will have a temporal offset, bounded by a period of one  frame with the lowest FPS
The above assumes that there are no frame drops.

in another issue (#384 ) I wrote a class to optimize finding the nearest neighbors using the word rather than its vector... i'll copy it here for reference:

```
class FastTextNN:
    
    def __init__(self, ft_model, ft_matrix=None):
        self.ft_model = ft_model        
        self.ft_words = ft_model.get_words()
        self.word_frequencies = dict(zip(*ft_model.get_words(include_freq=True)))
        self.ft_matrix = ft_matrix
        if self.ft_matrix is None:
            self.ft_matrix = np.empty((len(self.ft_words), ft_model.get_dimension()))
            for i, word in enumerate(self.ft_words):
                self.ft_matrix[i,:] = ft_model.get_word_vector(word)
    
    def find_nearest_neighbor(self, query, vectors, n=10,  cossims=None):
        """
        query is a 1d numpy array corresponding to the vector to which you want to
        find the closest vector
        vectors is a 2d numpy array corresponding to the vectors you want to consider

        cossims is a 1d numpy array of size len(vectors), which can be passed for efficiency
        returns the index of the closest n matches to query within vectors and the cosine similarity (cosine the angle between the vectors)

        """
        if cossims is None:
            cossims = np.matmul(vectors, query, out=cossims)

        norms = np.sqrt((query**2).sum() * (vectors**2).sum(axis=1))
        cossims = cossims/norms
        result_i = np.argpartition(-cossims, range(n+1))[1:n+1]
        return list(zip(result_i, cossims[result_i]))

    def nearest_words(self, word, n=10, word_freq=None):
        result = self.find_nearest_neighbor(self.ft_model.get_word_vector(word), self.ft_matrix, n=n)
        if word_freq:
            return [(self.ft_words[r[0]], r[1]) for r in result if self.word_frequencies[self.ft_words[r[0]]] >= word_freq]
        else:
            return [(self.ft_words[r[0]], r[1]) for r in result]
```

Actually, the part about `-fvisibility=hidden` being required might not be true anymore (as of #995 / 2.2.0): we now force hidden visibility on everything in the `pybind11` namespace (since #995), so it *should* actually be safe to compile modules without `-fvisibility=hidden`.  So that's another option to consider (by changing the cmake `CXX_VISIBILITY_PRESET` for the target to `default` rather than `hidden` that the default pybind cmake code sets).

The error is happening because the default holder for pybind classes is `std::unique_ptr`, which can't be converted to a `shared_ptr`.  2.1 added detection and failure for this; I'm not entirely sure what would have happened in 2.0 (perhaps a double free?).

The easiest fix is to change the holder on the registered types to `shared_ptr` with:

```C++
py::class_<Base, std::shared_ptr<Base>>(...);
py::class_<Derived, Base, std::shared_ptr<Derived>>(...);
```

(On a separate note, you'll want to make sure `Base` has a virtual destructor for anything more than a trivial class.)

The PR seems reasonable.  Note that adding the move ctor./assignment op implicitly deletes the default copy ctor./assignment op (which is good; this shouldn't be copyable).  It might be worthwhile making this explicit in the PR by adding:

```C++
buffer_info(const buffer_info &) = delete;
buffer_info &operator=(const buffer_info &) = delete;
```

(although that's already the current implicit behaviour with a move constructor or move assignment operator, it has the advantage of giving a more direct compilation error if you try to copy).

I think I was confused before when you said changing it to this worked:

`a = std::move(ndarray.acquire());`

because I can't see how that will be any different from:

`a = ndarray.acquire();`

assuming `acquire()` is returning by value: it's *already* an rvalue, so the `std::move` shouldn't change anything.  I'm pretty sure it's adding the move constructor and assignment operator that fixes it, not the `std::move`.

You should consider finding means to measure available memory before and during these runs, and as you change these values observe how memory usage changes. My guess is, at some point your system simply refuses to allocate more. If you get an `OSError` in Python, you are getting it from the underlying system. This may of course be limited to the space of an individual process. There may be a constraint in place on how much memory any unpriv'd process can alloc before it is denied, or could well just be a system limitation. The nightly builds are hosted independently on S3 and are generated for each commit on the master branch. You're not wasting github's resources, but instead resources of the person who's providing the S3 bin (@beardypig).

I'm strongly against uploading nightlies as regular github releases (even if tagged as a pre release), because it's not well designed for this use case. Most, if not almost all casual users who are downloading Windows installers from the github release pages do not know what they are downloading, because they are not reading the entire page. This means that uploading potentially unstable nightlies, although flagged as a pre release, could lead to bad user experiences if something doesn't work correctly (yet). It's a much better idea to use the github releases for only stable releases with a proper versioning schema and a change log.

As you can see in #167, there's a list of all nightlies named by commit number of the master branch and and shorthand ID of the commit (eg. 7c0db8c: `streamlink-0.5.0-1214-7c0db8c.exe`):
https://streamlink-builds.s3.amazonaws.com/index.html
https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/commits/master

Btw, you can see the explicit changes in the git log of the master branch, so there's really no need to download nightly builds every day unless it directly affects your usage of Streamlink, which is very unlikely.
If you still really want to install the latest development version every day, you could instead use python-pip and install Streamlink from the git repo and install all of its dependencies automatically:
`pip install git+https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink.git`

On Arch Linux, the `streamlink-git` AUR package can be installed, which is the equivalent of this or the nightly Windows installer. Does chocolatey allow development packages? Maybe this could be another solution for Windows users.

@arigo points out something disturbing about Python threads (on both CPython and PyPy): `thread.join()` doesn't actually call `pthread_join` (or I assume the equivalent on windows, but I haven't checked). It just waits for the thread to say "hey I'm about to exit". (And all threads are marked as "detached" from pthread's point of view.) So in theory there is a race condition where we could end up with arbitrarily many threads in the last stages of exiting, that are taking up an arbitrary amount of address space. (And then of course `run_in_worker_thread` also recreates this problem; it doesn't call `join` at all, just assumes that the worker thread will exit between when the thread schedules the callback to the trio thread and when the callback releases the limiter. But if `join` worked we could fix this by calling `join` from the callback, just before releasing the limiter.)

I *think* the practical impact of this problem is very low. In particular, 64-bit systems have the address space to handle an almost arbitrary number of threads, and even on 32-bit systems, if our default thread limit is on the order of "dozens", then it would take a *lot* of threads simultaneously stalled in the last bit of exiting to cause a problem. Unless the heap was taking up most of the address space, I guess. It makes me a bit nervous that it's the sort of thing that someone will find a way to hit under high load though.

One response to this would be to use a thread pool. We would want an unbounded thread pool, I think, i.e. one where if a job comes in and all threads are busy, we spawn a new thread (and if a thread is idle for too long, it goes away). (So that's an interesting control problem to avoid too much churn.) So long as worker threads marked themselves as "soon to be available" before signaling back to the trio thread, this would avoid the race condition in the steady state case by making sure that we never have more threads running than were allowed at some point by the trio-side limiting within the last N seconds. In principle it though would still be possible to hit the race condition by having exactly the wrong sort of cyclic workload, where we spin up N threads, then they all sit idle long enough to start running their cleanup logic, and while they're still doing that we suddenly have to spin up another N threads to replace them, so we end up with 2*N threads running at once.

To solve this really-properly-for-sure, we would need to stop using Python's threading module to spawn and join threads. Unfortunately, it's not quite as simple as using cffi to wrap `pthread_spawn`/`pthread_join` or the Windows equivalents, because to *properly* spawn a thread on CPython you have to take care of subinterpreters, which means passing through the threadstate from the parent to the child. I guess potentially we could try whining at Armin or someone to fix it so that ABI-mode callbacks can remember a particular tstate and then restore it when they're invoked.

Given our unbounded thread pool semantics, it makes sense to use a single thread pool for the whole process (shared across subinterpreters and threads), which might simplify things, or make them more complicated. It's not mandatory.

This is a good exercice as it forces me to take some distance from the PyEcore code, improve it, and improve the textX integration at the same time. In this context, I added an easier way of manually creating 'static' PyEcore metaclasses (the code generation is still the prefered and recommanded way), you can directly write:

```python
class A(EObject, metaclass=MetaEClass):
    name = EAttribute(eType=EString)
```
to create a new `EClass`. This is fine, but still a little but verbose, so I introduced a class decorator that allows you to do this instead (it uses the same mecanisme than the previous example):

```python
@metaclass
class A(object):
    name = EAttribute(eType=EString)
```
This looks really fine, but I cannot say if this is a good or a bad idea. I know that Mike is not a big fan of decorators for some usage (and for good reasons), but I cannot say in this context. The first mecanism still works, it is just some syntactic sugar.

What do you think?
